1.synthesis: On a Sabbath, Jesus and his disciples walk through grain fields; the disciples pluck heads of grain and eat, rubbing them in their hands. Some Pharisees object: "Why are you doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath?" (vv.1–2). Jesus cites David eating the bread of the Presence (reserved for priests) when he and his companions were hungry, from 1 Samuel 21:1–6 (vv.3–4). "The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath" (v.5). On another Sabbath, a man with a withered right hand is in the synagogue; the scribes and Pharisees watch to see if Jesus will heal on the Sabbath, to find a charge against him (vv.6–7). Jesus knows their thoughts and asks the man to stand in the middle. "I ask you, is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm, to save life or to destroy it?" (v.9). He looks at them all and says to the man: "Stretch out your hand." It is restored. The Pharisees and scribes discuss with each other what they might do to Jesus (vv.10–11).
1.historical_context: The Sabbath controversies reflect real disputes within Second Temple Judaism about the boundaries of Sabbath law. Rabbinic tradition codified extensive Sabbath restrictions in the Mishnah tractate Shabbat; the Pharisaic school of Shammai was generally more restrictive, Hillel more lenient. The objection to grain-plucking was likely based on the Exodus 34:21 provision against reaping on the Sabbath. David's eating of the bread of the Presence (1 Samuel 21:1–6) was a case of need overriding priestly restriction, which Jesus cites as a precedent. Luke's note that it was the man's "right hand" (v.6) that was withered is a characteristic Lukan detail (a physician's specificity); the right hand was the principal working hand, so the restoration was also a restoration of livelihood.
1.literary_structure: The two Sabbath controversies form a diptych: the grain-plucking (vv.1–5) centers on the Son of Man's lordship over the Sabbath's purpose; the withered-hand healing (vv.6–11) centers on the question of what the Sabbath is for (good or harm, life or destruction). Both arguments have the same logic: the Sabbath's purpose is human flourishing; any interpretation that prevents good for human beings has inverted the Sabbath's meaning. The escalation from grumbling (vv.1–5) to active plotting (v.11: "they discussed with one another what they might do to Jesus") prefigures the Passion. Luke places both controversies before the choosing of the Twelve (6:12–16), suggesting the opposition is established before the mission community is formed.
1.theological_application: "The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath" (v.5). The Sabbath was established at creation (Genesis 2:2–3) as God's own rest, given to Israel as the sign of the Sinai covenant (Exodus 31:12–17), and described in Deuteronomy 5:12–15 as commemorating the liberation from Egypt. The claim that the Son of Man is its lord is a claim to divine authority over the creation ordinance itself. The Sabbath's purpose — rest, liberation, human wholeness — is precisely what Jesus enacts by healing the withered hand on the Sabbath: the Sabbath that frees from slave labor in Deuteronomy is the Sabbath that frees from the slavery of disability in Jesus's ministry.
1.christology: "Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm, to save life or to destroy it?" (v.9). The question is rhetorical but precise: doing nothing while someone suffers is not morally neutral — it is choosing harm. Jesus's restoration of the man's hand on the Sabbath is the Sabbath-purpose enacted: the day of liberation liberating a man from the withered hand that has disabled him. That the Pharisees leave to discuss how to destroy Jesus while he heals on a day of rest shows the exact inversion Jesus's question named: they will do harm on the Sabbath (plotting destruction) in order to prevent him from doing good. The Lord of the Sabbath is recognized by what he does on the Sabbath: save life, restore, liberate.

12.synthesis: "In these days he went out to the mountain to pray, and all night he continued in prayer to God. And when day came, he called his disciples and chose from them twelve, whom he named apostles" (vv.12–13). The twelve are listed: Simon (Peter), Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot who became a traitor (vv.14–16). The all-night prayer before the choice is unique to Luke — consistent with his pattern of framing every major decision of Jesus's mission in prayer. The number twelve echoes the twelve tribes of Israel; the apostles will be the foundation of the renewed Israel that Jesus is constituting.
12.historical_context: The term "apostle" (apostolos — "sent one") appears rarely in the other Gospels (Matthew and Mark use it once each) but is characteristic of Luke-Acts; it distinguishes the twelve as commissioned representatives rather than simply followers. The list of the Twelve varies slightly across the Gospels: Luke has "Judas the son of James" where Matthew and Mark have "Thaddaeus." Simon "the Zealot" is variously interpreted as a former member of the Zealot nationalist movement (which sought violent overthrow of Rome) or as characterized by religious zeal more broadly. The presence of both Matthew the tax collector (collaborator with Rome) and Simon the Zealot (at minimum deeply anti-Roman) in the same group of twelve is one of the most striking social facts about Jesus's community: the kingdom gathered the politically irreconcilable.
12.literary_structure: The choosing of the Twelve follows the Sabbath controversies and precedes the Sermon on the Plain — Luke's sequence differs from Mark's (where the Twelve are chosen earlier) by placing the choosing at a pivotal moment of escalating opposition and before the community's founding discourse. The all-night prayer (v.12) frames the choice as a divinely guided decision: Jesus does not choose the Twelve on the basis of human assessment alone but after sustained communion with the Father. The list's final entry — "Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor" (v.16) — plants the shadow of the Passion within the community's founding moment.
12.theological_application: That Jesus prayed all night before choosing the Twelve does not mean the choice was uninformed by observation and human wisdom — it means the choice was ultimately submitted to divine direction. The founding of the apostolic community is an act of prayer-grounded discernment rather than administrative organization. The twelve chosen include those who will deny (Peter), doubt (Thomas), and betray (Judas) — the community is not constituted by the worthiness of its members but by the call of its Lord. This is the template for the church's own self-understanding: called by Christ, not self-selected on the basis of qualification.
12.christology: Jesus constitutes a community of twelve at a mountain after a night of prayer — the parallels with Moses receiving the law on Sinai for the twelve tribes are deliberate. Jesus is the new Moses who gives the new law (the Sermon on the Plain follows immediately) to the reconstituted Israel he is gathering. But Jesus does more than Moses: Moses received and transmitted the divine word; Jesus speaks the new law on his own authority ("But I say to you" — 6:27). The night of prayer preceding the choice places the constitution of the apostolic community within the Father-Son relationship: the twelve are the Father's gift to the Son (John 17:6), chosen through the Son's prayer.

17.synthesis: Jesus comes down with the Twelve and stands on a level place with a great crowd from all Judea, Jerusalem, and the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon (v.17). He heals many and begins to teach his disciples: "Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you shall laugh. Blessed are you when people hate you, on account of the Son of Man" (vv.20–22). He adds four corresponding Woes: "But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation. Woe to you who are full now, for you shall be hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep. Woe to you when all people speak well of you" (vv.24–26). Luke's four beatitudes and four woes are addressed in the second person, rooted in concrete material circumstances, and paired with eschatological reversals.
17.historical_context: Luke's Beatitudes (four, second person, on a level place) differ markedly from Matthew's (eight, third person, on a mountain — Matthew 5:3–12). Luke's "blessed are you who are poor" (ptōchoi — the literally destitute) is more concrete than Matthew's "poor in spirit." The woes have no parallel in Matthew's Sermon on the Mount; they are an original Lukan feature that creates the symmetrical structure of blessing and curse. The OT background of the Beatitudes includes the social critique of the prophets (Isaiah 61:1-2; Amos 5-6) and the Psalter's voice for the poor (Psalm 34:6; Psalm 37:11). The crowd includes Gentile regions (Tyre and Sidon) — the sermon is addressed to a mixed audience from the beginning.
17.literary_structure: The four beatitudes and four woes are structured as antithetical pairs: poor/rich, hungry/full, weeping/laughing, persecuted/praised. Each pair has two poles: a present condition reversed in the future (poor now → kingdom of God; rich now → consolation received) and a future reversal (the present suffering will be ended; the present comfort will be ended). The future tenses of the beatitudes ("you shall be satisfied," "you shall laugh," "your reward is great in heaven") place the fullness of blessing in the eschatological future while affirming it as certain. The Woes are not curses but warnings: the present sufficiency of the wealthy is all they will receive — the eschatological future holds not more comfort but reversal.
17.theological_application: "Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God" (v.20). The present-tense claim on the kingdom by the poor does not mean wealth is sinful in itself, but that material poverty creates the conditions for kingdom receptivity that wealth tends to foreclose. The woes address the spiritual danger of sufficiency: those who are already comforted, full, and laughing have satisfied their hungers with what the present age provides — they are not hungry for what the kingdom offers. This is Luke's consistent economic theology: wealth is a spiritual risk because it replaces dependence on God with self-sufficiency.
17.christology: "Blessed are you when people hate you and exclude you and revile you and spurn your name as evil, on account of the Son of Man!" (v.22). The identification of persecution with the Son of Man — suffering because of one's association with Jesus — places the Beatitudes in the redemptive-historical framework. The disciples who are poor, hungry, weeping, and hated for Jesus's sake are participating in the pattern that Jesus himself embodies: the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head (9:58), is rejected by his hometown, and will be crucified. Their beatitude is not merely consolation but participation in Christ's own pattern: the way of the cross leads to resurrection, and the way of poverty and rejection leads to the kingdom.

27.synthesis: "But I say to you who hear, Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you" (vv.27–28). Turn the other cheek; give your tunic to one who takes your cloak; give to everyone who asks; do not demand back what is taken (vv.29–30). The Golden Rule: "Do to others as you would have them do to you" (v.31). What credit is it to love those who love you, to do good to those who do good to you? Even sinners do the same (vv.32–34). "But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return, and your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil. Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful" (vv.35–36).
27.historical_context: Enemy-love in the ancient world was not simply counterintuitive; it was countercultural. Greek and Roman ethical tradition generally endorsed proportional retaliation and the obligation to help friends and harm enemies. The Stoic tradition counseled restraint from emotion but not active love of enemies. Qumran's Community Rule instructed members to "love all the sons of light" and "hate all the sons of darkness." Jesus's command is therefore radical in its social and ethical context: it inverts the friend/enemy distinction that structured both Jewish sectarian ethics and Greco-Roman social relations. The rationale — imitation of God who "is kind to the ungrateful and the evil" (v.35) — grounds the ethical imperative in the character of God rather than in social reciprocity.
27.literary_structure: The section is structured by the contrast between two logics: the logic of reciprocity (love those who love you = the tax collector standard) and the logic of grace (love those who hate you = the Son of the Most High standard). The core ethical commands (vv.27–30) are followed by the Golden Rule (v.31) and then by the reciprocity critique (vv.32–34), which in turn leads to the positive reformulation of enemy-love as divine imitation (vv.35–36). Luke's version ends with "Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful" (v.36) rather than Matthew's "Be perfect, as your heavenly Father is perfect" (Matthew 5:48) — mercy rather than perfection as the governing divine attribute, consistent with Luke's characteristic emphasis.
27.theological_application: "You will be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil" (v.35). The ethical imperative of enemy-love is grounded in ontology — the disciples who love their enemies are living according to the character of the God who is their Father. Family resemblance to God is expressed in treating the ungrateful and evil the way God does: with generosity not conditioned on their response. This is not merely a moral demand but a description of what the new human being looks like — the image of God (imago Dei) restored in the disciple through the practice of divine-pattern love. The Golden Rule (v.31) summarizes the neighbor-love dimension; the enemy-love command extends it beyond the boundary that human nature draws.
27.christology: "Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful" (v.36). Jesus teaches the disciples to pray to the Father using the Abba prayer (ch. 11); here he teaches them to live as Father-like in their ethics. The bridge between the teaching and the life is Jesus himself: the one who loves his enemies is Jesus, who will pray from the cross "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do" (23:34). Enemy-love is not an abstract principle in Luke but a concrete practice visible in the teacher — the disciples are called to imitate what they will see him do at Calvary. The cross is the ultimate enemy-love, and the commands of Luke 6:27–36 describe the community that forms around the one who bears it.

37.synthesis: "Judge not, and you will not be judged; condemn not, and you will not be condemned; forgive, and you will be forgiven; give, and it will be given to you. Good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be put into your lap. For with the measure you use it will be measured back to you" (vv.37–38). A blind man cannot lead another blind man; both fall into a pit (v.39). A disciple is not above his teacher — when fully trained, like the teacher (v.40). "Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye but do not notice the log in your own eye?" (vv.41–42). First remove the log from your own eye; then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's eye. The metaphorical logic is consistent: the measure of judgment one extends to others will be returned in the same measure; the hypocrite who judges while blind to his own sin cannot serve the brother he would correct.
37.historical_context: The prohibition against judging (krino) in v.37 is not a prohibition of all evaluation or discernment — Jesus himself makes numerous evaluative statements throughout Luke — but of the condemning judgment that usurps God's role as the final evaluator of human worth and standing. The marketplace imagery of v.38 ("pressed down, shaken together, running over... in your lap") reflects the measuring of grain: merchants would press down, shake, and heap the measure to give maximum value. This is applied to the divine economy of generosity: the standard of giving that humans extend to others will be exceeded by God's return measure. The log/speck (Greek: dokos/karphos — beam/splinter) is one of Jesus's characteristic comic hyperboles: the man trying to extract a splinter from another's eye while carrying a roof-beam in his own.
37.literary_structure: The section moves from prohibition (do not judge/condemn) to positive command (forgive/give), from the measure-returned maxim (v.38) to the parable of the blind leading the blind (v.39) to the teacher-disciple relationship (v.40) to the log-and-speck diagnosis (vv.41–42). The common thread is the theme of perception: judgment is impaired when the judge is blind, over-critical, or self-deceived. The disciple-teacher relationship in v.40 is a christological hinge: when a disciple is fully trained, he will be "like his teacher" — the ethical teaching of the sermon is formation into the character of Jesus, not a moral code enforced by outside pressure.
37.theological_application: "First take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck that is in your brother's eye" (v.42). The point is not that correction of others is forbidden but that self-examination must precede it. The log is not an excuse to avoid helping the brother; it is a prerequisite to helping him effectively. Moral vision is clouded by self-righteousness; only the self-examined person can see another's situation clearly enough to address it honestly. This is the pastoral ethic: the corrector must be under correction, the counselor must be known and knowing, the judge must stand under judgment. Hypocrisy in spiritual direction is not merely a personal failing but a blindness that makes the direction useless.
37.christology: "A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher" (v.40). The goal of discipleship in Luke is not information about Jesus but formation into the likeness of Jesus — the teacher's own character is the curriculum. The ethical demands of the Sermon on the Plain (love enemies, forgive, give generously, remove the log first) are not arbitrary commands but descriptions of how Jesus himself behaves. The disciple becomes like the teacher by following the teacher: the Sermon is both commandment and promise — it describes what Jesus does and what disciples-in-formation become through following him.

43.synthesis: "No good tree bears bad fruit, nor again does a bad tree bear good fruit, for each tree is known by its own fruit. Figs are not gathered from thornbushes, nor are grapes picked from a bramble bush. The good person out of the good treasure of his heart produces good, and the evil person out of his evil treasure produces evil, for out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks" (vv.43–45). "Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and not do what I tell you?" (v.46). The two builders: everyone who comes to Jesus and hears his words and does them is like a man who built a house on rock — when the flood came, it could not shake it; the one who hears and does not do is like a man who built on earth without a foundation — the moment the stream broke against it, the house fell (vv.47–49). The Sermon on the Plain ends with the foundation metaphor: obedience to Jesus's teaching is the difference between structural integrity and catastrophic collapse.
43.historical_context: The tree/fruit image was a common Jewish metaphor for moral life (cf. Psalm 1:3; Jeremiah 17:8; Proverbs 11:30). The builders parable has two versions: Luke's man builds "on the earth without a foundation," specifically a dry-weather construction exposed when floodwaters come (v.49); Matthew's man builds "on sand" (Matthew 7:26). Palestinian houses built on wadi banks or flood-prone ground were vulnerable to sudden winter flash floods; building on bedrock was the mark of a careful builder. The double address "Lord, Lord" (v.46) implies familiarity — those who say it have heard Jesus's teaching and use his title — but their non-obedience reveals a disconnect between verbal acknowledgment and life-orientation.
43.literary_structure: The Sermon's conclusion moves from character (tree and fruit, vv.43–45) to confession (Lord, Lord, v.46) to construction (builders, vv.47–49). The connecting logic: true character produces observable fruit (not merely religious vocabulary); those who confess without doing are revealed as tree-and-fruit mismatches; the builders parable gives the structural consequence of the mismatch — collapse when tested. The sermon thus ends not with blessing but with a warning about the test that will come: the house will be flooded, the foundation tested, the structure's integrity revealed. In Luke's narrative context, the test comes at the Passion — the disciples who build on Jesus's word will stand; those who don't will fall.
43.theological_application: "For out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks" (v.45). Words are the product of the heart, not the cause of it — no amount of "Lord, Lord" vocabulary transforms a heart that has not been reorganized around Jesus's commands. The Sermon on the Plain does not prescribe behaviors to be performed externally but describes the character of the person whose heart has been transformed by the kingdom's arrival. The tree's health determines the fruit; the heart's treasure determines the speech; the foundation determines the structure's survival. This is the Lukan ethic of formation: not rules imposed from outside but the life that flows from a heart reoriented toward the one calling himself Lord.
43.christology: "Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and not do what I tell you?" (v.46). The question presupposes that Jesus's commands have the authority of the Lord's commands — that obeying him and not doing what he says are weighty matters, not merely social preferences. The builders who come to Jesus and hear his words and do them (v.47) have Jesus himself as their foundation: the rock is not an abstract principle but the teacher whose words they obey. Matthew's Sermon ends with the centurion and crowd astonished because Jesus teaches with authority and not as the scribes — Luke's Sermon ends with Jesus calling himself the Lord whose commands are the foundation of life. Both point to the same claim: this teacher is the one on whom the whole structure stands.
